nativeRange.habitat: Broken coral rubble interspersed with outcrops of soft and stony corals; benthopelagic; depth 20–38 m

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on live copepods, amphipods, and other small benthic invertebrates in the wild. In captivity may accept baby brine shrimp, frozen bloodworms, glassworms, and frozen Mysis; however many specimens initially refuse prepared foods and require a dense live copepod population in the display tank. A connected refugium is strongly recommended. Tank should be well-established before introduction.

avoidWith: other male dragonets (conspecific males will fight)
fast-moving competitive feeders (damsels, aggressive gobies, dottybacks)

summary: A vivid, tiny reef dragonet from the Philippines with a bright red body and yellow belly; generally considered reef-safe and peaceful, but tends to require a mature tank with a thriving copepod population.
careNotes:
The Ruby Red Dragonet (Synchiropus sycorax) is a small Callionymidae dragonet described in 2016, reaching roughly 4 cm SL in males (females smaller at ~2.7 cm SL). It has been reported to accept a wider range of prepared foods than the Green Mandarin (Synchiropus splendidus), though many newly acquired specimens initially refuse frozen foods and need live copepods and amphipods to sustain them. A refugium producing a continuous supply of pods is generally advised for long-term success.

Only one male should be kept per display; males are territorial toward conspecifics. A single male can be housed with one or more females. Multiple females may be introduced simultaneously if the tank is large enough to support them all.

This species has been documented as a jumper — a tightly secured, escape-proof lid is strongly recommended. The Reef2Reef community noted jumping behavior explicitly.

Like other scaleless dragonets in Callionymidae, this species is considered highly sensitive to copper-based medications and should never be treated with copper.

Tank size rationale: LiveAquaria lists a 30-gallon (113 L) minimum, which is likely a retailer-conservative entry-level figure. A 55-gallon equivalent (~200 L) is used here to better support the live pod population needed for sustainable long-term feeding.

Size cross-source note: FishBase records males at 4.0 cm SL / females at 2.7 cm SL; LiveAquaria states 3 inches (7.6 cm TL) maximum. SL vs. TL conversion accounts for some of this gap; average across sources gives roughly 5–6 cm TL for males. Range of 2–6 cm used to span female to full male adult size.

Temperature cross-source note: LiveAquaria states 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); general tropical Callionymidae parameters suggest 24–27°C. Range of 22–26°C adopted as a conservative averaged range.

Also sometimes sold as 'Ruby Scooter Dragonet' or 'Ruby Dragonet' in the trade.
